INGREDIENTS:
<ul>
<li>fresh lime juice (1 oz)</li>
<li>rock candy syrup (0.25 oz)</li>
<li>orgeat (0.25 oz)</li>
<li>orange curacao (0.5 oz)</li>
<li>aged Martinique agricole (1 oz)</li>
<li>aged pot-still Jamaican rum (1 oz)</li>
<li>crushed ice (1 cup)</li>
<li>lime shell (1)</li>
<li>mint sprig (1)</li>
</ul>

HEADNOTES:
<aside class="callout callout-note" role="note"><p>Vic claimed he invented the drink at his Oakland bar in 1944 and christened it after his Tahitian friend Carrie Guild took a sip and exclaimed "Mai Tai-Roa Aé" — "out of this world, the best."<sup class="footnote-ref" id="fnref-1"><a href="#fn-1">1</a></sup> Donn Beach later asserted he had served a Mai Tai at his Hollywood Don the Beachcomber's as early as 1933, and the two men feuded publicly over authorship; modern historiography generally credits Vic with the drink as it is known today (the rum-orgeat-curaçao-lime sour), while acknowledging Donn's earlier and structurally unrelated namesake.<sup class="footnote-ref" id="fnref-2"><a href="#fn-2">2</a></sup> Vic's 1944 spec called for a single 17-year J. Wray & Nephew Jamaican rum;<sup class="footnote-ref" id="fnref-3"><a href="#fn-3">3</a></sup> when that stock dried up in the 1950s Vic himself reformulated with a blend of Jamaican and Martinique rums, and modern reconstructions follow that split-base approach to approximate the lost rum's profile.<sup class="footnote-ref" id="fnref-4"><a href="#fn-4">4</a></sup> The Mai Tai's structural bones are the Jamaican planter's-punch sour tradition (one of sour, two of sweet, three of strong, four of weak) — it descends from West Indian punch, not Polynesian invention.<sup class="footnote-ref" id="fnref-5"><a href="#fn-5">5</a></sup></p><p>Brand recommendations. Best: Smith & Cross or Hamilton Pot Still Black for the Jamaican; Rhum J.M. VSOP or Clément VSOP for the agricole; Pierre Ferrand Dry Curaçao; Small Hand Foods or BG Reynolds orgeat. Economical: Appleton Estate 8 + Plantation 3 Stars (or Denizen Merchant's Reserve as a single-bottle stand-in); Cointreau; Liber & Co. orgeat. Single-bottle alternative: Denizen Merchant's Reserve, an 8-year blend of Plummer-style Jamaican pot-still rum and Martinique rhum grand arôme. It was developed by Nick Pelis with Martin Cate of Smuggler's Cove specifically to fill the slot of the lost J. Wray & Nephew 17, approximating the blend Vic himself reached for in the "second adjusted" Mai Tai recipe after the original Jamaican rum stock dried up. Pour 2 oz of Merchant's Reserve in place of the split rum bill.</p></aside>

INSTRUCTIONS:
<ol>
<li>In a <span class="cookware">shaker</span> combine <span class="ingredient" data-amount="1" data-unit="oz">fresh lime juice</span>, <span class="ingredient" data-amount="0.25" data-unit="oz">rock candy syrup</span>, <span class="ingredient" data-amount="0.25" data-unit="oz">orgeat</span>, <span class="ingredient" data-amount="0.5" data-unit="oz">orange curacao</span>, <span class="ingredient" data-amount="1" data-unit="oz">aged Martinique agricole</span>, and <span class="ingredient" data-amount="1" data-unit="oz">aged pot-still Jamaican rum</span>.</li>
<li>Add <span class="ingredient" data-amount="1" data-unit="cup">crushed ice</span> and shake until chilled, about <button type="button" class="timer" data-value="10" data-unit="seconds" aria-label="Start 10 seconds timer">10 seconds</button>.</li>
<li>Pour unstrained into a <span class="cookware">double old-fashioned glass</span>.</li>
<li>Garnish with the spent <span class="ingredient" data-amount="1" data-unit="">lime shell</span> and a <span class="ingredient" data-amount="1" data-unit="">mint sprig</span>.</li>
</ol>

FOOTNOTES:
<section class="recipe-footnotes" aria-label="Sources"><h2 class="font-serif text-2xl mb-6">Sources</h2><ol><li id="fn-1">Bergeron, <em>Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide, Revised</em> (1972), pp. 163–164 — Vic's own creation narrative, including the "Mai Tai-Roa Aé" christening by Carrie Guild and the closing line "Anybody who says I didn't create this drink is a dirty stinker." Vic was self-promotional and the narrative should be read as advocacy, not neutral history; cited here for the christening anecdote, which is corroborated nowhere else but uncontested. <a href="#fnref-1" class="footnote-backref" aria-label="Back to text">↩</a></li><li id="fn-2">Berry, <em>Sippin' Safari</em> (2017 ed.), Mai Tai chapter; Cate & Cate, <em>Smuggler's Cove</em> (2016), Mai Tai entry — both document the Vic / Donn Beach authorship dispute and conclude that the modern Mai Tai (rum-orgeat-curaçao-lime sour) is Vic's, while Donn's earlier "Mai Tai" was a different drink that shared only the name. <a href="#fnref-2" class="footnote-backref" aria-label="Back to text">↩</a></li><li id="fn-3">Berry, <em>Sippin' Safari</em> (2017 ed.), Mai Tai chapter — documents the 17-year J. Wray & Nephew Jamaican rum as the original 1944 base and traces the rum's disappearance from the market. <a href="#fnref-3" class="footnote-backref" aria-label="Back to text">↩</a></li><li id="fn-4">Cate & Cate, <em>Smuggler's Cove</em> (2016), Mai Tai entry — describes the modern split-base reconstruction (aged Jamaican + aged Martinique agricole) and the Denizen Merchant's Reserve collaboration with Nick Pelis to recreate the lost J. Wray & Nephew profile in a single bottle. <a href="#fnref-4" class="footnote-backref" aria-label="Back to text">↩</a></li><li id="fn-5">Curtis, <em>And a Bottle of Rum</em> (2006), planter's-punch chapter — traces the West Indian planter's-punch tradition ("one of sour, two of sweet, three of strong, four of weak") that underlies the Mai Tai's structural template. <a href="#fnref-5" class="footnote-backref" aria-label="Back to text">↩</a></li><li id="fn-6">Beachbum Berry, "Mai Tai." https://beachbumberry.com/recipe-mai-tai.html (accessed 2026-05-03) — Berry's published reconstruction of the 1944 spec, the source for this file's measurement bill.</li></ol></section>
